title: SCORE — Assistant Manager / Manager Assessment
audience: Assistant Managers and Managers overseeing BIM coordination teams
description: Purely HR/behavioral instrument, 30 questions at ~90 seconds each. Technical/software skill is assessed separately (technical test + Personal Interview), so this test carries no technical items at all. Weighted for a managerial role: Leadership gets the most questions, Communication and Team Play next, then Problem-Solving; Dispute Resolution and Organizational Mindset carry smaller dedicated quotas (though still higher than at the modeler level, reflecting real managerial exposure), and Ethics is a minimal but always-active dimension — ethics-concern flags can surface on any question, not just the 2 dedicated ones.

sections.instructions: Choose the option that best reflects how you would actually act. There is no single 'correct' answer — pick the one truest to your instinct.

questions.prompt: A junior staff member makes a significant error that reaches the client before being caught. As team lead, what's your response?

options.text: Correct the mistake quietly yourself and hold off telling the client, since raising it might create embarrassment that doesn't seem worth the risk.
Explain to the client that the junior staff member was responsible, keeping the focus off your own role in overseeing the work.
Own the error with the client, share the correction and prevention plan, then address the root cause privately and constructively with the person involved.
Decide the mistake is serious enough that the junior staff member should be let go from the team without further discussion.

questions.prompt: A major clash is discovered right before a permit submission and your team is panicking. What's your leadership approach?

options.text: Step in and take over every task yourself, feeling that's the only way to be confident everything gets handled correctly under pressure.
Stay calm, quickly triage the issue, delegate clear ownership of specific parts, and set a tight but realistic timeline with regular check-ins.
Step back and let the team work through it on their own, mainly to avoid coming across as micromanaging during a stressful moment.
Before diving into the fix, spend some time figuring out who's responsible for the clash so accountability is clear going forward for everyone.

questions.prompt: A talented team member consistently disrespects deadlines but produces excellent technical work. How do you manage this?

options.text: Let the missed deadlines slide since the technical work is strong enough that it doesn't seem worth risking friction over timing.
Address the deadline pattern directly and specifically, set clear expectations and consequences, while still acknowledging their genuine technical strength.
Call out the repeated lateness in front of the whole team so everyone understands deadlines are taken seriously here.
Quietly shift their tasks over to other people instead of raising the deadline pattern directly with them in conversation.

questions.prompt: A new Assistant Manager under you is eager but making decisions above their authority level, causing confusion on the team. How do you handle it?

options.text: Correct their decisions openly in front of the team whenever they overstep, so everyone sees where the real authority sits.
Have a direct, supportive conversation clarifying decision boundaries, mentor them on judgment, and reinforce those boundaries consistently over time.
Say nothing directly to them, but quietly override the decisions yourself whenever you happen to notice them stepping too far.
Pull back their decision-making authority without much explanation, figuring the situation will sort itself out over time.

questions.prompt: Productivity data (billed hours vs. deliverables) shows one team member underperforming for several months. What's your approach as manager?

options.text: Quietly adjust that person's billed hours after the fact, reasoning it's simpler than raising the performance issue with them or the client.
Have a private, evidence-based performance conversation, identify root causes, set a clear improvement plan with support, then follow up.
Let the underperformance continue for now, since raising it feels like it would create an uncomfortable conversation you'd rather avoid.
Quietly shift their work over to others rather than having a direct conversation with them about the performance gap.

questions.prompt: You're promoted to manage former peers. One of them resents reporting to you now. What do you do?

options.text: Hold back from giving them much direction for now, hoping the resentment fades naturally if you just keep the peace.
Have an honest, direct conversation acknowledging the shift, clarify expectations, and earn credibility through fair, consistent leadership over time.
Assert your new authority firmly and early, making clear that reporting lines have changed regardless of how they feel about it.
Mention their attitude to your own manager rather than addressing it with them directly yourself in the moment.

questions.prompt: A high-priority client project needs a lead assigned from your team, and two capable people want the role. How do you decide?

options.text: Give the role to whoever has pushed for it most persistently, since that seems like the clearest signal of interest.
Assess based on project fit, availability, and development opportunity, explain the reasoning transparently to both, and offer the other a near-term opportunity.
Step back from deciding and let the two of them work out between themselves who should take the lead role.
Choose based on your own personal preference, without bothering to explain that reasoning clearly to either person involved.

questions.prompt: A client is unhappy about slipped deadlines and threatens to escalate to your director. What's your approach?

options.text: Hold off on returning the client's calls for now, until you have more concrete progress or better news to share with them.
Reach out to the client proactively, acknowledge the delay honestly, and walk them through a realistic recovery plan with firm dates.
When speaking with the client, frame the delay as mostly driven by dependencies on other disciplines and subcontractors outside your direct control.
Give the client a recovery date that feels reassuring in the moment, even though you're not fully confident the team can hit it.

questions.prompt: A client asks directly whether your team can meet an aggressive deadline you privately doubt is realistic. What do you say?

options.text: Tell the client yes for now to avoid disappointing them, planning to work out the actual feasibility with the team later.
Give an honest assessment of the risks and what would need to be true to hit the date, then propose realistic options.
Tell the client directly that the deadline isn't realistic, without offering any alternative path or compromise in the moment.
Keep your answer vague and noncommittal, hoping to avoid pinning yourself down until you have more clarity later.

questions.prompt: A client says your own communication style in status meetings is 'too technical and hard to follow.' How do you respond?

options.text: Brush off the feedback, reasoning that technical accuracy in the meetings matters more than making things simpler for the client.
Take the feedback seriously, adjust your communication approach for that audience, and check in regularly for ongoing feedback afterward.
Get a little defensive and explain to the client why they should try to follow your explanations more closely.
Hand off future client meetings to someone else on the team rather than working on adjusting your own approach.

questions.prompt: Budget cuts mean reducing your team's software license count, which will slow some work. How do you communicate this internally?

options.text: Announce the license cut without much explanation, figuring the team just needs to adjust to the new reality quickly.
Explain the business context honestly, involve the team in prioritizing the affected workflows, and set realistic expectations with stakeholders.
Frame the cut as something imposed vaguely by 'management above,' rather than owning the decision yourself as their leader.
Hold off telling the team about the change until the reduced license count actually starts causing visible problems.

questions.prompt: You need to deliver difficult news to the whole team — a valued project was cancelled or scope significantly reduced. How do you approach it?

options.text: Send the team a short email about the change and steer clear of further discussion unless someone directly asks.
Hold a live conversation, explain the context honestly, acknowledge the impact on the team, and answer their questions directly.
Let word of the change spread informally through the team rather than addressing it directly yourself in a meeting.
Downplay how significant the change really is when talking to the team, even if that stretches the truth somewhat.

questions.prompt: Two people on your team interpret your instructions for a task completely differently, causing duplicated/conflicting effort. What do you do?

options.text: Point out to the two of them that they should have asked more clarifying questions before running with their own interpretation.
Take ownership of the miscommunication, clarify the task immediately, and adjust your future instructions to be more explicit going forward.
Leave the two of them to sort out the conflicting interpretations on their own rather than stepping in yourself.
Just go with whichever interpretation is more convenient at this point, without circling back to explain the mix-up.

questions.prompt: Two previously siloed discipline sub-teams under you rarely collaborate and duplicate effort. How do you build a stronger sense of shared team identity?

options.text: Leave the two sub-teams as they are for now, since each one is still delivering its own work reasonably well.
Create shared touchpoints like joint reviews and shared recognition, and actively model cross-team collaboration yourself in day-to-day work.
Merge the two sub-teams formally on paper, without really addressing the underlying culture that's kept them siloed apart.
Hold off addressing the disconnect unless a client actually complains about duplicated effort or inconsistent coordination between the two groups.

questions.prompt: A star performer on your team refuses to mentor or share knowledge with others, protecting their own indispensability. What do you do?

options.text: Leave them be for now, since their individual output is strong enough that it seems not worth pushing on.
Talk with them directly about how mentoring factors into how you evaluate senior-level contributions, and set up structured chances for them to mentor.
Call them out in front of the team for not being enough of a team player about sharing knowledge.
Quietly shift mentoring responsibilities onto other people instead of addressing the underlying behavior with them directly yourself.

questions.prompt: Your team must absorb extra scope with no extra time, and morale is already stretched. How do you rally them?

options.text: Just assign the extra scope to the team and trust everyone to manage it on top of their current workload.
Be transparent about the situation, involve the team in deciding how to divide the load fairly, and acknowledge their extra effort.
Take on most of the extra work yourself quietly, rather than asking an already-stretched team to absorb more of it.
Promise the team rewards for the extra push that you're not entirely sure you'll actually be able to deliver.

questions.prompt: A remote or satellite-office team member feels excluded from key decisions made in the main office. What do you do?

options.text: Assume it's a fairly minor issue, since the remote team member still ends up receiving the final decisions either way.
Actively include them in relevant discussions going forward and check in regularly on how included they actually feel.
Suggest that they bring it up themselves in a meeting if the exclusion is really bothering them that much.
Hold off addressing it unless their output actually starts to suffer as a visible result of feeling excluded.

questions.prompt: A Junior and a Senior on the same task are competitive with each other rather than collaborative, and it's affecting quality. What do you do?

options.text: Let the two of them work it out between themselves, since a bit of healthy competition can push quality up too.
Set clear shared goals and structure the task so their success is interdependent, addressing the competitive dynamic directly with both.
Move them onto separate tasks going forward instead of addressing the competitive dynamic between them directly in conversation.
Reprimand both of them for the friction without spending much time understanding what's actually driving the competitive dynamic.

questions.prompt: A team lead under you wants to skip a QA review step to hit a deadline, 'just this once.' What's your call?

options.text: Approve skipping QA this one time, reasoning the deadline pressure justifies it and it's unlikely to cause real problems.
Say no, but work with the team to find a genuinely faster path to complete QA properly rather than skipping it.
Approve the request without much discussion, since you generally trust the lead's judgment on calls like this one.
Say no to skipping QA, but don't offer any alternative way to still try to meet the deadline.

questions.prompt: A client's facilities team insists on a design change that conflicts with a code-compliance issue your BIM team identified. What do you do?

options.text: Go ahead with the client's requested change, reasoning that they're paying for the project and it's ultimately their call.
Clearly document the code conflict and risk, get the decision formally recorded, and escalate internally and to the client's leadership before implementing anything.
Implement the change as requested, but quietly have a junior document it as a CYA measure rather than raising it with the client formally.
Refuse to discuss the code conflict with the client any further, treating the matter as effectively already closed.

questions.prompt: Your team can hit the deadline OR maintain full quality, but not both, and the client hasn't been made aware of this trade-off. What's your approach as manager?

options.text: Quietly cut some quality to hit the deadline, reasoning the client doesn't need to know about the trade-off being made.
Present the trade-off honestly to the client and let them decide which matters more, or negotiate for more time or resources.
Miss the deadline without giving the client any advance warning, hoping the quality outcome will speak for itself later.
Push the team to work unsustainable hours trying to hit both targets, without really addressing the systemic scheduling issue.

questions.prompt: Two competing internal priorities — a strategic new client and an existing client's ongoing work — both need your best people at the same time. How do you resolve the resourcing conflict?

options.text: Give the new strategic client whatever resourcing they ask for, since landing them well feels like the higher priority.
Evaluate objectively against business priorities, communicate the trade-offs transparently to both stakeholders, and look for creative resourcing options first.
Default to whichever client is currently pushing hardest or complaining the loudest about needing more resourcing support.
Split your best people evenly across both, regardless of what each project's actual staffing needs really are.

questions.prompt: A long-standing process at your studio clearly isn't working anymore as the studio has grown, but changing it will be disruptive short-term. What do you do?

options.text: Leave the process alone for now, since changing something that's embedded this deeply feels risky and disruptive short-term.
Propose and pilot a change with a clear plan to manage the short-term disruption, gathering team input before rolling it out.
Force the change through right away without piloting it or consulting the team about how it'll actually affect their work.
Point out repeatedly that the process is outdated, without actually putting together a concrete proposal to fix it.

questions.prompt: Two department leads under you have an ongoing personal conflict that's starting to affect team morale and coordination quality. What do you do?

options.text: Stay out of it for now, treating the tension between the two leads as more of a personal matter.
Meet with each separately, then together, understand the root causes, establish clear working agreements, and follow up over time.
Address the tension between them openly in a team meeting so everyone understands it needs to stop right away.
Transfer one of them to a different team fairly quickly, without really digging into what's driving the conflict.

questions.prompt: A dispute escalates between your team and another team (led by a peer manager) over who's responsible for a coordination failure that affected the client. What do you do?

options.text: Frame the coordination failure to the client as mainly the other team's responsibility, to protect your own team's standing.
Meet privately with the peer manager first, focus on root cause and preventing recurrence rather than blame, then align on a joint message.
Avoid engaging directly with the other manager and let the client draw their own conclusions about what happened.
Escalate the disagreement straight to senior leadership rather than attempting to resolve it peer-to-peer with the other manager first.

questions.prompt: Two of your direct reports have a disagreement about technical approach that's becoming personal rather than professional. What do you do?

options.text: Let the two of them work it out, since stepping in might make it look like you're taking sides.
Intervene early, separate the technical disagreement from the personal friction, mediate a resolution, and address the interpersonal tension directly.
Side with whichever one is more senior in the discussion, mainly to bring the disagreement to a quicker end.
Reassign one of them to a different task, mainly as a way to sidestep dealing with the conflict directly.

questions.prompt: You notice a pattern where multiple projects at your studio hit avoidable delays for the same root-cause reason. What's your response as manager?

options.text: Handle each project's delay on its own as it comes up, without really stepping back to look at the pattern.
Bring the recurring pattern to studio leadership, push for a root-cause fix, and follow up on whether it actually moves outcomes across projects.
Assume other managers are likely noticing the same pattern too and will probably raise it themselves eventually.
Mention the pattern informally in passing to a few people, without really pushing for a concrete fix afterward.

questions.prompt: A decision that's good for your team's short-term numbers would be mildly harmful to the studio's reputation with a client relationship that matters long-term. What do you do?

options.text: Prioritize your team's short-term numbers, reasoning that's mainly what you're measured on day to day as a manager.
Weigh the long-term studio and client relationship appropriately, even if it means a weaker short-term outcome, and explain that reasoning upward.
Make the short-term-favorable choice for your team without really raising or considering the longer-term angle with anyone.
Avoid making the call for now and hope the situation somehow resolves itself without your direct involvement.

questions.prompt: Senior management asks you to reduce hours reported to a client without reducing the actual work required, to preserve a fee cap. What do you do?

options.text: Go along with it since it's a direct instruction from senior management, and questioning it doesn't feel worth the friction.
Push back clearly, explain the ethical and legal risk of misreporting hours, and propose alternatives like scope renegotiation or an efficiency review.
Go along with the instruction for now, but quietly keep your own private record of it in case questions come up later.
Quietly decide not to follow the instruction, without ever actually raising your concerns about it directly with management.

questions.prompt: You discover a deliverable sent to a client last week contained a significant, uncaught modeling error affecting cost estimates. What do you do?

options.text: Hold off saying anything for now, to see whether the client happens to notice the error on their own first.
Proactively notify the client as soon as possible, explain the correction clearly, and review internal QA to prevent recurrence.
Quietly fix the error in the next deliverable, without flagging that the earlier version had actually contained a mistake.
When informing the client, attribute the error mainly to the software or an external vendor rather than your own team.
